items.q: What absorber is needed to stop beta radiation?
items.correct: A few millimetres of aluminium (or similar thin metal sheet).
items.misconception: A sheet of paper — beta is only slightly more penetrating than alpha.
items.random_wrong: Thick lead — only lead can stop any radiation.
items.why: Students remember 'paper, aluminium, lead' as a trio but mix up which goes with which radiation type.

items.q: Is radioactive decay periodic — does each nucleus decay at regular intervals?
items.correct: No — decay is a random process; half-life is a statistical property of a large population.
items.misconception: Yes — each nucleus 'ticks' at intervals of one half-life until it decays.
items.random_wrong: Yes — half-life is the time between each emission from the source.
items.why: Students treat half-life as a clock for individual atoms instead of a probability-based property of bulk samples.

items.correct: No — its photon energy is below the threshold required to ionise atoms; ionising radiation includes UV (high energy), X-rays and gamma.
items.misconception: Yes — all electromagnetic radiation is ionising because it carries energy.
items.random_wrong: Yes — only infrared and radio waves are non-ionising.
items.why: Students don't link photon energy (E = hf) to the ability to ionise, so they treat all EM radiation as equivalent in damage potential.

items.q: Which radiation has the greatest penetrating power?
items.why: It's Gamma. “Alpha” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: 2 protons, 2 neutrons. “1 proton, 1 neutron” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If a source emits 800 counts/min and background is 20 counts/min, what is the corrected count?
items.why: Exact answer: 780 counts/min. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
